challenges.title: Print a greeting
challenges.description: Print the text 'Hello, World!' to the console.
challenges.hint: Use println() with a string argument.

steps.title: Understand the entry point
steps.explanation: Kotlin programs start from the top-level main() function. No class wrapper is required.

steps.title: Print to the console
steps.explanation: println() writes a line followed by a newline to standard output.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: Run the program. You should see 'Hello, World!' in the output.

challenges.title: Null-safe length
challenges.description: Write a function `safeLength(s: String?): Int` that returns the length of the string, or 0 if the string is null.
challenges.hint: Use the Elvis operator ?: to provide a default value.

steps.title: Safe call operator
steps.explanation: s?.length returns the length if s is not null, or null if s is null.

steps.title: Elvis operator
steps.explanation: ?: provides a default value when the left side is null.

steps.title: Full solution
steps.explanation: Combine both operators in a single expression.

challenges.title: Flatten a nested list
challenges.description: Write a function `flatten(nested: List<List<Int>>): List<Int>` that returns a single flat list containing all elements from all inner lists.
challenges.hint: The standard library has flatMap. You can also use fold with plus.

solution.code: fun flatten(nested: List<List<Int>>): List<Int> = nested.flatMap { it }

steps.title: Use flatMap
steps.explanation: flatMap transforms each element and flattens the result into a single list. Passing the identity { it } simply concatenates all inner lists.

steps.title: Full solution
steps.explanation: One line using the standard library.
steps.code: fun flatten(nested: List<List<Int>>): List<Int> = nested.flatMap { it }

challenges.title: Sealed class state machine
challenges.description: Model a simple traffic light using a sealed class `TrafficLight` with states Red, Yellow, Green. Write a function `next(light: TrafficLight): TrafficLight` that transitions Red→Green→Yellow→Red.
challenges.hint: Declare the states as data objects inside the sealed class. Use a when expression in next().

steps.title: Define states as data objects
steps.explanation: Sealed class subclasses are limited to the same file or class. data object gives each state a meaningful toString() and equals().

steps.title: Exhaustive when
steps.explanation: When used as an expression, Kotlin's when must be exhaustive. The compiler verifies that every state is handled.
